1:
"And Moses went up from the plains of Moab unto the mountain of Nebo, to the top of Pisgah... And the LORD shewed him all the land of Gilead, unto Dan," The witnesses note Moses ascending Nebo, and the LORD showing him all the promised land. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses went up from the plains of Moab, where he had repeated the law, sung the song, and blessed the tribes, at the command of God. The witnesses note that the LORD showed him all the land of Gilead unto Dan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went up to Nebo — the dying servant ascending; the to the top of Pisgah — the place of vision; the the LORD shewed him — all the land displayed; the seen afar off — the promise viewed though not possessed (Heb. 11:13).

The witnesses agree the closing scene opens with God's tender grace to his dying servant: Moses climbed Nebo, and "the LORD shewed him all the land" he had longed to enter; though denied the entrance, he was granted the sight, God himself displaying the whole inheritance to his eyes; the showing of the land he might not tread mingles judgment with mercy, the servant honoured with a vision of the promise; it teaches that God often gives his faithful a sight of the good things they shall not here possess, a foretaste granted from the mount, "these all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off" (Heb. 11:13).

So the witnesses make the verse shown the land: The witnesses note the went up to Nebo: the dying servant ascending. The witnesses note the to the top of Pisgah: the place of vision. The witnesses note the the LORD shewed him: all the land displayed. The witnesses note the seen afar off: the promise viewed though not possessed (Heb. 11:13). The expositors observe the went up to Nebo: the dying servant ascending. The expositors observe the to the top of Pisgah: the place of vision. The expositors observe the the LORD shewed him: all the land displayed. The expositors observe the seen afar off: the promise viewed though not possessed (Heb. 11:13). The believer is taught to mark the went up to Nebo, the to the top of Pisgah, the the LORD shewed him, the seen afar off, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Moses ascended Nebo and the LORD showed him all the land — judgment mingled with mercy, the servant granted the sight he might not enter, seeing the promise afar off (Heb. 11:13).
2:
"And all Naphtali, and the land of Ephraim, and Manasseh, and all the land of Judah, unto the utmost sea," The witnesses note the sweep of the vision — the whole land from north to the western sea shown to Moses. The witnesses note that Gill notes Naphtali, in the north, where was Galilee of the Gentiles, most frequented by the Messiah when come. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the lands of Ephraim, Manasseh, and Judah unto the utmost sea. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all Naphtali — the north, Galilee of the Gentiles; the Ephraim and Manasseh — the central tribes; the all Judah — to the utmost sea; the the whole inheritance — the land of promise surveyed.

The witnesses agree the vision granted Moses embraced the whole extent of the land, tribe by tribe, to the farthest sea: "all Naphtali... Ephraim... Manasseh... all the land of Judah, unto the utmost sea"; God spread the entire inheritance before his servant's eyes, that he might see in full what he had laboured forty years to reach; Gill notes that Naphtali, "Galilee of the Gentiles," would be most frequented by the Messiah — a quiet pointing forward; it teaches the completeness of God's gift and the graciousness of the sight given, the dying leader shown the whole land of promise, the labour of his life surveyed from the height.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole land: The witnesses note the all Naphtali: the north, Galilee of the Gentiles. The witnesses note the Ephraim and Manasseh: the central tribes. The witnesses note the all Judah: to the utmost sea. The witnesses note the the whole inheritance: the land of promise surveyed. The expositors observe the all Naphtali: the north, Galilee of the Gentiles. The expositors observe the Ephraim and Manasseh: the central tribes. The expositors observe the all Judah: to the utmost sea. The expositors observe the the whole inheritance: the land of promise surveyed. The believer is taught to mark the all Naphtali, the Ephraim and Manasseh, the all Judah, the the whole inheritance, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Moses was shown the whole land, tribe by tribe, to the farthest sea — the completeness of God's gift, the dying leader granted to survey the land of promise, Galilee of the Gentiles where the Messiah would walk.
3:
"And the south, and the plain of the valley of Jericho, the city of palm trees, unto Zoar." The witnesses note the southern land and the plain of Jericho, the city of palm trees, completing the view. The witnesses note that Gill notes the southern part of the land, all of it, and then the view directed eastward. The witnesses note that Ellicott identifies the south (the Negeb), the plain of Jordan, and the valley of Jericho, the city of palm trees. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the south — the Negeb shown; the the plain of Jericho — the gateway of conquest; the the city of palm trees — the first to be taken; the the survey complete — no part of the land unshown.

The witnesses agree the survey was complete, reaching even to the south and the plain of Jericho: "the south... the plain of the valley of Jericho, the city of palm trees, unto Zoar"; God left no part of the land unshown, the whole promised possession laid before Moses from end to end; the naming of Jericho, the first city Israel would take, set the gateway of the conquest before his eyes; it teaches that God's grace to his servant was full, the entire land granted to his sight, the very threshold of the inheritance shown to him who had led the people to its borders but might not cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the south and Jericho: The witnesses note the the south: the Negeb shown. The witnesses note the the plain of Jericho: the gateway of conquest. The witnesses note the the city of palm trees: the first to be taken. The witnesses note the the survey complete: no part of the land unshown. The expositors observe the the south: the Negeb shown. The expositors observe the the plain of Jericho: the gateway of conquest. The expositors observe the the city of palm trees: the first to be taken. The expositors observe the the survey complete: no part of the land unshown. The believer is taught to mark the the south, the the plain of Jericho, the the city of palm trees, the the survey complete, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Moses was shown the south and the plain of Jericho — the survey complete, the whole land from end to end laid before him, the gateway of the conquest set before his eyes.
4:
"And the LORD said unto him, This is the land which I sware unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, saying, I will give it unto thy seed: I have caused thee to see it with thine eyes, but thou shalt not go over thither." The witnesses note God naming the land as the fulfilment of his oath to the fathers, yet telling Moses he shall not cross. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Word of the Lord, as the Jerusalem Targum, having shown him all the land. The witnesses note that Ellicott sets the oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob now shown fulfilled to Moses' eyes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the land I sware — the oath to the fathers; the I have caused thee to see — the promise shown fulfilled; the but thou shalt not go over — the sentence held firm; the the law sees but enters not — joshua leads in (Heb. 4:8).

The witnesses agree the LORD's word seals both his faithfulness and his sentence: "this is the land which I sware unto Abraham... I have caused thee to see it... but thou shalt not go over"; God points to the fulfilment of his ancient oath, the promise now visibly kept, yet holds firm the sentence barring Moses for the fault at Meribah; the mingling of the kept promise with the withheld entrance shows that God is faithful to his word though he chastens his servant; it teaches that the law (in Moses) brings the people to the very border of the rest and sees the promise fulfilled, yet cannot itself bring in — only Joshua, the true Joshua, leads in (cf. Heb. 4:8).

So the witnesses make the verse this is the land: The witnesses note the the land I sware: the oath to the fathers. The witnesses note the I have caused thee to see: the promise shown fulfilled. The witnesses note the but thou shalt not go over: the sentence held firm. The witnesses note the the law sees but enters not: Joshua leads in (Heb. 4:8). The expositors observe the the land I sware: the oath to the fathers. The expositors observe the I have caused thee to see: the promise shown fulfilled. The expositors observe the but thou shalt not go over: the sentence held firm. The expositors observe the the law sees but enters not: Joshua leads in (Heb. 4:8). The believer is taught to mark the the land I sware, the I have caused thee to see, the but thou shalt not go over, the the law sees but enters not, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that God named the land as his oath fulfilled, yet Moses might not cross — his faithfulness and his sentence together, the law bringing to the border and seeing the promise, yet only Joshua leading in (Heb. 4:8).
5:
"So Moses the servant of the LORD died there in the land of Moab, according to the word of the LORD." The witnesses note the death of Moses, the servant of the LORD, in Moab, at God's word. The witnesses note that Gill notes he died on a mountain in the land of Moab, contrary to some Jewish writers who deny he died; he died according to the word of the LORD. The witnesses note that he is honoured with the title "the servant of the LORD." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses died — in the land of Moab; the the servant of the LORD — his crowning title; the according to the word — by God's appointment; the a faithful end — well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21).

The witnesses agree the death of Moses is recorded with peculiar honour: "Moses the servant of the LORD died... according to the word of the LORD"; the title "servant of the LORD," given as his epitaph, crowns his life of faithful service, no higher honour than to be owned God's servant; his dying "according to the word of the LORD" — by God's appointment, some say by his kiss — marks the peaceful, obedient end of a faithful life; it teaches that to die in the LORD's service and at his word is the blessed end of his own, "well done, thou good and faithful servant" (Matt. 25:21), Moses honoured to the last as God's servant.

So the witnesses make the verse the servant died: The witnesses note the Moses died: in the land of Moab. The witnesses note the the servant of the LORD: his crowning title. The witnesses note the according to the word: by God's appointment. The witnesses note the a faithful end: well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21). The expositors observe the Moses died: in the land of Moab. The expositors observe the the servant of the LORD: his crowning title. The expositors observe the according to the word: by God's appointment. The expositors observe the a faithful end: well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21). The believer is taught to mark the Moses died, the the servant of the LORD, the according to the word, the a faithful end, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Moses, the servant of the LORD, died at God's word — honoured with that crowning title, the peaceful end of a faithful life, well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21).
6:
"And he buried him in a valley in the land of Moab, over against Beth-peor: but no man knoweth of his sepulchre unto this day." The witnesses note the LORD himself burying Moses in a secret grave, unknown to any man. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jarchi and other Jewish writers say the Lord buried him, perhaps by the ministry of angels; though he died on the mount, he was buried in a valley. The witnesses note that no man knows his sepulchre to this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he buried him — the LORD himself, by angels; the in a valley — over against Beth-peor; the no man knoweth — the grave hidden; the against idolatry — the body kept from abuse (Jude 9).

The witnesses agree the burial of Moses was a singular honour and a hidden mystery: "he buried him" — the LORD himself, it is held, by the ministry of angels — "but no man knoweth of his sepulchre"; the secret grave, the witnesses note, was perhaps so hidden lest Israel make an idol of it, the body kept from superstitious honour; the apostle Jude records that Michael "disputed about the body of Moses" (Jude 9); it teaches that God honours his servants even in death, yet guards against the abuse of their memory, the grave of Moses hidden by the same God who buried him, that the people worship the LORD and not his servant.

So the witnesses make the verse the secret grave: The witnesses note the he buried him: the LORD himself, by angels. The witnesses note the in a valley: over against Beth-peor. The witnesses note the no man knoweth: the grave hidden. The witnesses note the against idolatry: the body kept from abuse (Jude 9). The expositors observe the he buried him: the LORD himself, by angels. The expositors observe the in a valley: over against Beth-peor. The expositors observe the no man knoweth: the grave hidden. The expositors observe the against idolatry: the body kept from abuse (Jude 9). The believer is taught to mark the he buried him, the in a valley, the no man knoweth, the against idolatry, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that the LORD buried Moses in a secret grave, unknown to any — a singular honour, the grave hidden lest his memory be abused, God guarding against idolatry even of his servant (Jude 9).
7:
"And Moses was an hundred and twenty years old when he died: his eye was not dim, nor his natural force abated." The witnesses note Moses dying at a hundred and twenty, his sight and strength unimpaired to the end. The witnesses note that Gill notes his age divided into three equal periods — forty years in Pharaoh's court, forty in Midian, forty in the government of Israel; the common age of man then being lower. The witnesses note that his eye was not dim nor his force abated. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a hundred and twenty — his long life; the his eye not dim — sight unimpaired; the force not abated — strength unbroken; the kept vigorous — fruitful in old age (Ps. 92:14).

The witnesses agree the preservation of Moses' vigour to the last was a mark of God's special grace: at a hundred and twenty "his eye was not dim, nor his natural force abated"; his long life, fallen into three forties — court, exile, and leadership — was crowned with an undimmed eye and unbroken strength, no decay of age upon him; the keeping of his powers whole shows how God can sustain his servants in full vigour to their appointed end; it teaches that the faithful may be kept fresh and strong to the close, "they shall still bring forth fruit in old age; they shall be fat and flourishing" (Ps. 92:14), Moses' force unabated to the day of his death.

So the witnesses make the verse eye not dim: The witnesses note the a hundred and twenty: his long life. The witnesses note the his eye not dim: sight unimpaired. The witnesses note the force not abated: strength unbroken. The witnesses note the kept vigorous: fruitful in old age (Ps. 92:14). The expositors observe the a hundred and twenty: his long life. The expositors observe the his eye not dim: sight unimpaired. The expositors observe the force not abated: strength unbroken. The expositors observe the kept vigorous: fruitful in old age (Ps. 92:14). The believer is taught to mark the a hundred and twenty, the his eye not dim, the force not abated, the kept vigorous, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Moses died at a hundred and twenty, his eye undimmed and force unbroken — God's grace sustaining his servant in full vigour to the appointed end, fruitful in old age (Ps. 92:14).
8:
"And the children of Israel wept for Moses in the plains of Moab thirty days: so the days of weeping and mourning for Moses were ended." The witnesses note Israel mourning Moses thirty days in the plains of Moab. The witnesses note that Gill notes, with Josephus and the Samaritan Chronicle, that they wept vehemently when he signified his death and took his leave, and mourned publicly when he was dead. The witnesses note that the days of mourning were thirty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wept for Moses — the people's grief; the thirty days — a full season of sorrow; the a fitting tribute — to their great leader; the the loss felt — great lamentation made (Acts 8:2).

The witnesses agree the long public mourning was the people's fitting tribute to their great leader: Israel "wept for Moses... thirty days," a full and solemn season of grief; the depth and length of their sorrow honoured the man who had borne them so long and led them so faithfully; the mourning shows the love the people bore him at the last, and the loss they felt in his departure; it teaches that the death of God's faithful servants is rightly mourned by his people, "devout men carried Stephen to his burial, and made great lamentation over him" (Acts 8:2), the loss of a good leader deeply felt.

So the witnesses make the verse thirty days' mourning: The witnesses note the wept for Moses: the people's grief. The witnesses note the thirty days: a full season of sorrow. The witnesses note the a fitting tribute: to their great leader. The witnesses note the the loss felt: great lamentation made (Acts 8:2). The expositors observe the wept for Moses: the people's grief. The expositors observe the thirty days: a full season of sorrow. The expositors observe the a fitting tribute: to their great leader. The expositors observe the the loss felt: great lamentation made (Acts 8:2). The believer is taught to mark the wept for Moses, the thirty days, the a fitting tribute, the the loss felt, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Israel mourned Moses thirty days — a fitting tribute of grief to their faithful leader, the death of God's servants rightly and deeply mourned by his people (Acts 8:2).
9:
"And Joshua the son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom; for Moses had laid his hands upon him: and the children of Israel hearkened unto him..." The witnesses note Joshua filled with the spirit of wisdom, succeeding Moses, and the people obeying him. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua, the successor of Moses, was abundantly qualified by the spirit of wisdom for the government of Israel, in which he was a type of Christ, on whom the spirit of wisdom rests. The witnesses note that Henry marks the succession and the people hearkening to him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the spirit of wisdom — joshua gifted for rule; the Moses laid his hands — the succession given; the the people hearkened — obeying the successor; the a type of Christ — the spirit of wisdom resting on him (Isa. 11:2).

The witnesses agree the passing of the leadership to Joshua, "full of the spirit of wisdom," shows God's gracious continuance of his care for his people beyond the death of Moses: the work did not fail with the leader, for a successor was raised up and gifted, and the people "hearkened unto him"; Gill marks Joshua a type of Christ, on whom the Spirit of wisdom rests without measure; it teaches that God provides for his church in every age, raising up leaders as the old pass away, and that Joshua — whose name is Jesus — leading the people into the rest Moses could not, foreshadows the true Joshua who brings his people home (cf. Isa. 11:2, the spirit of wisdom... shall rest upon him).

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua filled: The witnesses note the the spirit of wisdom: Joshua gifted for rule. The witnesses note the Moses laid his hands: the succession given. The witnesses note the the people hearkened: obeying the successor. The witnesses note the a type of Christ: the spirit of wisdom resting on him (Isa. 11:2). The expositors observe the the spirit of wisdom: Joshua gifted for rule. The expositors observe the Moses laid his hands: the succession given. The expositors observe the the people hearkened: obeying the successor. The expositors observe the a type of Christ: the spirit of wisdom resting on him (Isa. 11:2). The believer is taught to mark the the spirit of wisdom, the Moses laid his hands, the the people hearkened, the a type of Christ, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Joshua, full of the spirit of wisdom, succeeded Moses and the people obeyed — God's care continuing beyond the leader, Joshua a type of the true Joshua on whom the Spirit of wisdom rests, who brings his people in (Isa. 11:2).
10:
"And there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face," The witnesses note that no prophet arose like Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face. The witnesses note that Gill notes there arose not a prophet like Moses in all the Old Testament — not Joshua, Samuel, or any to the time of Ezra. The witnesses note that the LORD knew Moses face to face. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no prophet like Moses — unequalled in the law; the whom the LORD knew — face to face; the his nearness to God — above all who followed; the Christ exceeding him — moses a servant, Christ a son (Heb. 3:5-6).

The witnesses agree the closing testimony exalts Moses as unequalled among the prophets of the old covenant: "there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face"; the nearness of his communion with God — "face to face" — set him above all who followed under the law; yet the very uniqueness of Moses points beyond him, for the promised Prophet "like unto" him (Deut. 18:18) was none of the old prophets but the Messiah; the apostle marks that Moses was faithful as a servant, "but Christ as a son over his own house" (Heb. 3:5-6); it teaches that the greatest of the prophets was yet but a forerunner of the greater, Christ exceeding Moses.

So the witnesses make the verse none like Moses: The witnesses note the no prophet like Moses: unequalled in the law. The witnesses note the whom the LORD knew: face to face. The witnesses note the his nearness to God: above all who followed. The witnesses note the Christ exceeding him: Moses a servant, Christ a son (Heb. 3:5-6). The expositors observe the no prophet like Moses: unequalled in the law. The expositors observe the whom the LORD knew: face to face. The expositors observe the his nearness to God: above all who followed. The expositors observe the Christ exceeding him: Moses a servant, Christ a son (Heb. 3:5-6). The believer is taught to mark the no prophet like Moses, the whom the LORD knew, the his nearness to God, the Christ exceeding him, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that no prophet arose like Moses, known of the LORD face to face — unequalled under the law, yet pointing beyond himself to the greater Prophet, Moses faithful as a servant, Christ as a son (Heb. 3:5-6).
11:
"In all the signs and the wonders, which the LORD sent him to do in the land of Egypt to Pharaoh, and to all his servants, and to all his land," The witnesses note the signs and wonders Moses was sent to do in Egypt as a mark of his unequalled greatness. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Targums read "which the Word of the Lord sent him to do" — he who appeared in the bush and sent him to Egypt to work miracles. The witnesses note that these were done to Pharaoh, his servants, and his land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the signs and wonders — moses' mighty works; the the LORD sent him — his singular commission; the to Pharaoh and Egypt — the plagues witnessing God's power; the works confirming the messenger — a shadow of the greater (John 15:24).

The witnesses agree the greatness of Moses was attested by the unequalled signs and wonders he wrought: "all the signs and the wonders, which the LORD sent him to do in the land of Egypt"; no prophet of the old covenant was sent to such mighty works, the plagues upon Pharaoh and Egypt witnessing the power of God through his servant; Gill notes the Targum's reading, that it was "the Word of the Lord" — Christ at the bush — who sent him; it teaches that the works confirmed the messenger, the wonders of Egypt marking Moses' singular commission, yet all these but a shadow of the greater works of the greater Prophet, who did such miracles as none other man did (cf. John 15:24).

So the witnesses make the verse the signs in Egypt: The witnesses note the all the signs and wonders: Moses' mighty works. The witnesses note the the LORD sent him: his singular commission. The witnesses note the to Pharaoh and Egypt: the plagues witnessing God's power. The witnesses note the works confirming the messenger: a shadow of the greater (John 15:24). The expositors observe the all the signs and wonders: Moses' mighty works. The expositors observe the the LORD sent him: his singular commission. The expositors observe the to Pharaoh and Egypt: the plagues witnessing God's power. The expositors observe the works confirming the messenger: a shadow of the greater (John 15:24). The believer is taught to mark the all the signs and wonders, the the LORD sent him, the to Pharaoh and Egypt, the works confirming the messenger, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that Moses wrought unequalled signs in Egypt — the works confirming his singular commission, the wonders attesting God's power through him, yet a shadow of the greater works of the greater Prophet (John 15:24).
12:
"And in all that mighty hand, and in all the great terror which Moses shewed in the sight of all Israel." The witnesses note the mighty hand and great terror of Moses' works — the closing words of the Pentateuch. The witnesses note that Gill notes the mighty hand, in all done by his hand — stretched over the sea to divide it, smiting the rocks; and the great terror shown in the sight of all Israel. The witnesses note that this closes the book and the law of Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the that mighty hand — the works of his hand; the great terror — the awful displays of power; the in the sight of all Israel — witnessed by the people; the the law sealed — grace and truth by Christ (John 1:17).

The witnesses agree the book closes by sealing the unequalled majesty of Moses' ministry: "all that mighty hand, and... great terror which Moses shewed in the sight of all Israel"; the dividing of the sea, the smiting of the rock, the awful displays of God's power through his hand, set him above all; Gill notes the "mighty hand" of his works and the "great terror" of the miracles and the law-giving; thus the Pentateuch ends, the five books of Moses sealed with the testimony of his greatness — yet a greatness wholly from God, and itself a forerunner of the greater glory of Christ, "the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ" (John 1:17), the servant's work crowned and surpassed in the Son.

So the witnesses make the verse the mighty hand: The witnesses note the that mighty hand: the works of his hand. The witnesses note the great terror: the awful displays of power. The witnesses note the in the sight of all Israel: witnessed by the people. The witnesses note the the law sealed: grace and truth by Christ (John 1:17). The expositors observe the that mighty hand: the works of his hand. The expositors observe the great terror: the awful displays of power. The expositors observe the in the sight of all Israel: witnessed by the people. The expositors observe the the law sealed: grace and truth by Christ (John 1:17). The believer is taught to mark the that mighty hand, the great terror, the in the sight of all Israel, the the law sealed, and to look to the Prophet greater than Moses. The verse thus records that the book closes with the mighty hand and great terror of Moses' works — the Pentateuch sealed with the testimony of his unequalled greatness, a greatness from God and surpassed in Christ, the law by Moses, grace and truth by Jesus Christ (John 1:17).
